source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 8.2.2-8.2.3 LVM commands (pp. 134-135)

questions.q: Turn the partition /dev/sdb1 into an LVM physical volume.
questions.why: pvcreate initialises a disk or partition as an LVM physical volume.

questions.q: Create a volume group called volume1 from the physical volume /dev/sdb1.
questions.why: vgcreate <VG_name> <PV...> groups one or more PVs into a volume group.

questions.q: Create a 600M logical volume named VolLog1 in the volume group volume1.
questions.why: lvcreate -L sets the size and -n the name; the file system is then created on the new LV.

questions.q: What is the difference between lvcreate's -L and -l options?
options: -L sets an absolute size (K/M/G); -l sets a number of PEs or a percentage of capacity
-L sets the name; -l sets the size
-L is for VGs; -l is for LVs
questions.why: -L takes a size like 600M; -l takes a PE count or percentage (one PE = 4 MB by default).

questions.q: After 'lvcreate -n VolLog1 volume1', what is the device path of the new LV?
questions.why: LVs are named /dev/VG_name/LV_name, a soft link pointing to a /dev/dm-N device.

questions.q: Which command adds another physical volume to an existing volume group (extends it)?
questions.why: vgextend grows a VG by adding PVs; lvextend grows a logical volume instead.

questions.q: View detailed information about the physical volume /dev/sdb1.
questions.why: pvdisplay shows PV details; vgdisplay and lvdisplay do the same for VGs and LVs.
